plainMeaning: May the whole world be blessed, may every living being work for others' good, may all faults disappear, and may everyone everywhere be happy.
recitation: śivam astu sarva-jagataḥ, para-hita-niratā bhavantu bhūta-gaṇāḥ. doṣāḥ prayāntu nāśaṁ, sarvatra sukhī bhavatu lokaḥ.

wordByWord.etymology: sandhi of śivam (neut. nom./acc. of śiva, 'auspicious, benign, weal') + astu (3rd sg. imperative of √as, 'to be'), hence 'let there be weal'
wordByWord.notes: śiva here is the noun 'welfare/blessedness', not the deity; the benediction governs the genitive sarva-jagataḥ that follows

wordByWord.etymology: karmadhāraya of sarva ('all, whole') + jagat ('that which moves; the world, all creation', from √gam); genitive singular jagataḥ = 'of the world'
wordByWord.notes: genitive of advantage: 'weal for the whole world'

wordByWord.gloss: intent on the welfare of others
wordByWord.etymology: para ('other') + hita ('good, welfare', from √dhā/hi) → para-hita ('others' good'); + nirata ('devoted to, engaged in', past part. of ni-√ram); nom. pl. niratāḥ agreeing with bhūta-gaṇāḥ
wordByWord.notes: a tatpuruṣa: 'those engaged in others' welfare' — the verse's altruistic core

wordByWord.gloss: may they become / let them be
wordByWord.notes: anusvāra for class-nasal (bhavaṁtu = bhavantu), preserved as printed

wordByWord.etymology: bhūta ('being, creature', past part. of √bhū) + gaṇa ('group, multitude, host'); nom. pl. -gaṇāḥ
wordByWord.notes: subject of bhavantu; 'all classes of beings', read here as every jīva

wordByWord.etymology: doṣa ('fault, blemish, defect, ill', from √duṣ 'to spoil'); nom. pl. doṣāḥ
wordByWord.notes: doubly resonant: moral faults (the passions/kaṣāya) and bodily-worldly ills alike

wordByWord.etymology: pra ('forth, away') + √yā ('to go'); 3rd pl. imperative prayāntu, 'let them go forth (to)'
wordByWord.notes: governs the accusative nāśaṁ: 'go to destruction'

wordByWord.etymology: nāśa ('loss, perishing, annihilation', from √naś 'to perish'); accusative singular nāśam = goal of motion
sarva ('all') + the locative-adverbial suffix -tra ('in/at'); 'in all places'
from sukha ('happiness, ease') + possessive suffix -in → sukhin; nom. sg. masc. sukhī, 'possessing happiness'

wordByWord.gloss: may it be / let it be
wordByWord.notes: singular, agreeing with the collective lokaḥ

wordByWord.etymology: loka ('world, people, folk', from √lok 'to see/behold'); nom. sg. lokaḥ
wordByWord.notes: collective sense, 'the world of people'; printed with a period before it per the book's punctuation

literalTranslation: Let there be weal for the whole world; may the hosts of beings be devoted to others' welfare; may faults go to their destruction; everywhere may the world be happy.
idiomaticTranslation: May blessing rest upon all creation; may every living being delight in the good of others; may every fault and affliction utterly perish; and may the whole world, in every place, be happy.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After verse 44 promised that, when the Jina is worshipped, one's own obstacles dissolve and the mind grows serene, this stanza turns that personal fruit outward into a boundless blessing. The reciter no longer prays for self alone but for sarva-jagat — the entire moving world. Its four imperatives form a deliberate ladder: śivam astu (let there be weal) is the broad benediction; parahita-niratā bhavantu sharpens it into an ethical wish — that beings not merely receive good but actively pursue others' good; doṣāḥ prayāntu nāśaṁ removes the inner obstacle to that life; and sukhī bhavatu lokaḥ seals it with universal happiness. Note the careful number-shift: the plural imperatives (bhavantu, prayāntu) govern the many — the bhūta-gaṇāḥ and the doṣāḥ — while the closing bhavatu is singular, gathering all those many beings back into one undivided loka. The metre is the lilting Āryā of the appended maṅgala verses, and the stanza is the well-loved 'Śivamastu' that closes countless Jain recitations. Its movement is centrifugal: from the protected self of v.44, through fearless peace, out to the welfare of the whole cosmos — fittingly placed just before v.46's final homage to Śānti, the very embodiment of the peace here wished upon all.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse is a crystalline expression of maitrī — universal friendliness — the first of the four bhāvanās (maitrī toward all, pramoda toward the virtuous, kāruṇā toward the suffering, mādhyastha toward the wicked) that Jain practice cultivates. parahita-niratā restates the active face of ahiṁsā: non-harm completed as positive benevolence. doṣāḥ is doctrinally rich — read inwardly, the doṣas are the kaṣāyas (rāga and dveṣa, the passions) whose destruction is the path to liberation; read outwardly, they are the ills and calamities a protective smaraṇa is recited to avert. The stanza thus folds the hymn's protective purpose into its ethical telos: true safety is a world cleansed of fault and bound in mutual goodwill.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at a hymn's close, this verse trains the heart to end worship not in self-interest but in goodwill for every being. The practitioner can pause on each clause as a resolve: to seek others' good, to watch one's own doṣas toward extinction, and to wish happiness on all — turning a moment of prayer into a daily renewal of maitrī.

sources.title: Sanskrit Dictionary (Monier-Williams / collaborative) — entries for śiva, hita, nirata, doṣa, nāśa, loka
sources.consultedFor: Root meanings and nominal/verbal forms for śivam astu, parahita-nirata, doṣa, nāśa, loka, bhū-imperatives

sources.title: Wisdomlib — Sanskrit definitions of nirata, bhūta, gaṇa, sukhin
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of nirata ('devoted to'), bhūta-gaṇa ('host of beings'), and sukhin ('possessing happiness')

sources.title: Jain stotra / prayer anthologies on the 'Śivamastu sarva-jagataḥ' maṅgala verse
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the stanza as a maitrī/maṅgala benediction appended in the recitation tradition; universal-welfare reading

notes: Verse text follows the book verbatim, including the anusvāra spellings (ભવંતુ, પ્રયાંતુ, નાશં) and the period printed before 'લોકઃ'; these are preserved, not normalized. The metre is Āryā, as in the other appended Sanskrit maṅgala stanzas (vv.43–46), whose individual authorship differs from Nandiṣeṇasūri and is not asserted here. 'doṣāḥ' is deliberately rendered with double force (moral passions/kaṣāya and worldly ills), both readings being current; no single reading is forced. Some recited recensions add 'sadā' or vary the second pāda ('parahita-niratā bhavatu…'); the book's wording is followed here.
